subject: Physics
q: The magnetic potential due to a magnetic dipole at a point on its axis situated at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from its center is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The magnetic moment of the dipole is _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. (Given : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>μ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: The magnetic potential due to a magnetic dipole at a point on its axis situated at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from its center is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The magnetic moment of the dipole is _______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. (Given : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>μ</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
The formula to calculate the magnetic potential due to a magnetic dipole can be written as

V=μ04πMr2   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) and solve to calculate the required magnetic moment of the dipole.

1.5×10-5=10-7×M20×10-22⇒M=1.5×10-5×20×20×10-410-7⇒M=1.5×4=6 A m2

subject: Physics
q: A circular coil has moment of inertia <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> around any diameter and is carrying current to produce a magnetic moment of  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>Am</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. The coil is kept initially in a vertical position and it can rotate freely around a horizontal diameter. When a uniform magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> is applied along the vertical, it starts rotating around its horizontal diameter. The angular speed the coil acquires after rotating by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>60</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup></math> will be :
question: A circular coil has moment of inertia <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> around any diameter and is carrying current to produce a magnetic moment of  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>Am</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>. The coil is kept initially in a vertical position and it can rotate freely around a horizontal diameter. When a uniform magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> is applied along the vertical, it starts rotating around its horizontal diameter. The angular speed the coil acquires after rotating by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>60</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup></math> will be :
solution:
From energy conservation 

12Iω2=Un-Ut

   =-MBcos60°-(-MB)

MB2=12Iω2

20×42=12(08)m2

100=ω2

ω=10rad

subject: Physics
q: A small cube of side $1$ mm is placed at the centre of a circular loop of radius $10$ cm carrying a current of $2$ A. The magnetic energy stored inside the cube is $\alpha \times 10^{-14}$ J. The value of $\alpha$ is _______. ($\mu_o = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ Tm/A, $\pi = 3.14$)
question: A small cube of side $1$ mm is placed at the centre of a circular loop of radius $10$ cm carrying a current of $2$ A. The magnetic energy stored inside the cube is $\alpha \times 10^{-14}$ J. The value of $\alpha$ is _______. ($\mu_o = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ Tm/A, $\pi = 3.14$)
solution:
The magnetic field at the centre of the circular loop is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2R}$

Substituting the given values, $B = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2}{2 \times 0.1} = 4\pi \times 10^{-6}$ T

The magnetic energy density is $u = \dfrac{B^2}{2\mu_0}$

$u = \dfrac{(4\pi \times 10^{-6})^2}{2 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}} = \dfrac{16\pi^2 \times 10^{-12}}{8\pi \times 10^{-7}} = 2\pi \times 10^{-5}$ J/m$^3$

The volume of the small cube is $V = (1 \text{ mm})^3 = (10^{-3} \text{ m})^3 = 10^{-9}$ m$^3$

The magnetic energy stored inside the cube is $U = u \times V$

$U = 2\pi \times 10^{-5} \times 10^{-9} = 2\pi \times 10^{-14}$ J

Using $\pi = 3.14$, we get $U = 2 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-14} = 6.28 \times 10^{-14}$ J

Comparing with $\alpha \times 10^{-14}$ J, we get $\alpha = 6.28$

Answer: $6.28$

subject: Physics
q: The magnetic field existing in a region is given by $\vec{B}=0.2(1+2 x) \hat{k} \mathrm{~T}$. A square loop of edge $50 \mathrm{~cm}$ carrying 0.5 A current is placed in $x-y$ plane with its edges parallel to the $x-y$ axes, as shown in figure. The magnitude of the net magnetic force experienced by the loop is_____ $\mathrm{mN}$.
question: The magnetic field existing in a region is given by $\vec{B}=0.2(1+2 x) \hat{k} \mathrm{~T}$. A square loop of edge $50 \mathrm{~cm}$ carrying 0.5 A current is placed in $x-y$ plane with its edges parallel to the $x-y$ axes, as shown in figure. The magnitude of the net magnetic force experienced by the loop is_____ $\mathrm{mN}$.
solution:
Force on segment parallel to $\mathrm{x}$-axis will cancel each other. Hence $\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{net}}$ will be due to portion parallel to $y$-axis.
$\begin{aligned}
& F=0.5 \times 0.5 \times 6 \times 0.2-0.5 \times 0.5 \times 0.2 \times 5 \\
& =0.5 \times 0.5 \times 0.2 \\
& =0.25 \times 0.2 \\
& =50 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~N} \\
& =50 \mathrm{mN}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: As shown in the figure, two infinitely long, identical wires are bent by 90o and placed in such a way that the segments LP and QM are along the x - axis, while segments PS and QN are parallel to the y - axis. If OP=OQ=4cm, and the magnitude of the magnetic field at O is 10-4 T, and the two wires carry equal currents (see figure), the magnitude of the current in each wire and the direction of the magnetic field at O will be μ0=4π×10-7NA-2:
question: As shown in the figure, two infinitely long, identical wires are bent by 90o and placed in such a way that the segments LP and QM are along the x - axis, while segments PS and QN are parallel to the y - axis. If OP=OQ=4cm, and the magnitude of the magnetic field at O is 10-4 T, and the two wires carry equal currents (see figure), the magnitude of the current in each wire and the direction of the magnetic field at O will be μ0=4π×10-7NA-2:
solution:
As we know that the magnetic field due to a infinite wire at a distance of d is given as 

B=μ0i4πd

but there are two wires so that the net magnetic field is given as

B=2μ0i4πd

10-4=24π×10-7×i4π×4100

i=20A

subject: Physics
q: A uniform electric field and a uniform magnetic field are acting along the same direction in a certain region. If an electron is projected along the direction of the fields with a certain velocity then
question: A uniform electric field and a uniform magnetic field are acting along the same direction in a certain region. If an electron is projected along the direction of the fields with a certain velocity then
options: it will turn towards right of direction of motion.
it will turn towards left of direction of motion
solution:
$\vec{F}=-e[\vec{E}+\vec{v} \times \vec{B}]=-e \vec{E}$
$\vec{a}=-\frac{e \vec{E}}{m}$
$v(t)=v_{\circ}-\frac{e E}{m} t$

q: A deuteron and an alpha particle having equal kinetic energy enter perpendicular into a magnetic field. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">α</mi></msub></math> be their respective radii of circular path. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></msub><msub><mi>r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">α</mi></msub></mfrac></math> is equal to:
question: A deuteron and an alpha particle having equal kinetic energy enter perpendicular into a magnetic field. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">α</mi></msub></math> be their respective radii of circular path. solution:
r=mvqB=2mkqB
rdrα=mdmαqαqd=2421=2
Hence option (2).

q:
A long solenoid is formed by winding 70 turns cm-1 . If 2.0 A current flows, then the magnetic field produced inside the solenoid is ______________.

(µ0= 4π× 10–7 T m A–1)
question:
A long solenoid is formed by winding 70 turns cm-1 . If 2.0 A current flows, then the magnetic field produced inside the solenoid is ______________.

(µ0= 4π× 10–7 T m A–1)
solution:
Magnetic field inside solenoid =μoni

where n= Number of turns per unit length

n=7000 turns/m

Bsolenoid =μoni=4π×10-7×7000×2

=8×227×10-6×7×100

=176×10-4 T

subject: Physics
q: A regular polygon of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> sides is formed by bending a wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi></math> meter. If an electric current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is flowing through the sides of the polygon, the magnetic field at the centre of the polygon would be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______.
question: A regular polygon of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> sides is formed by bending a wire of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi></math> meter. If an electric current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is flowing through the sides of the polygon, the magnetic field at the centre of the polygon would be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______.
solution:
Direction of magnetic field due to all six sides will be in the same direction, therefore we can write 

B=6μ0I4πrsin30°+sin30°, where r=distance of the sides from the centre.

=610-7×4π3π3×3

=72×10-7 T

Hence, x=72.

A rectangular loop of sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>  and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> with current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is in Y-Z plane. The current is in anticlockwise sense with reference to negative X axis. Magnitude and direction of the torque is :
question: A uniform magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> acts along positive Y-direction. A rectangular loop of sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>  and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> with current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is in Y-Z plane. The current is in anticlockwise sense with reference to negative X axis. Magnitude and direction of the torque is :
solution:
Magnetic moment is given by, M→=iA→

=5×0.2×0.1-i^

=0.1-i^

τ→=M→×B→=0.1−i^×2×10−3j^

=2×10−4−k^ N m

subject: Physics
q: A charge particle moving in magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>,</mo></math> has the components of velocity along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> as well as perpendicular to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>. The path of the charge particle will be
question: A charge particle moving in magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mo>,</mo></math> has the components of velocity along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> as well as perpendicular to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>. The path of the charge particle will be
options: helical path with the axis perpendicular to the direction of magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>
helical path with the axis along magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>
straight along the direction of magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>
solution:
The formula to calculate the radius of the path followed by the charged particle under an external magnetic field can be written as

r=mvBq   ...1

The perpendicular component of velocity results in circular motion, while the parallel component results in linear motion. Hence, the path of the charged particle is helical in nature, the axis of which lies along the direction of the magnetic field.

subject: Physics
q: Two wires A &  B are carrying currents I1 and I2 as shown in the figure. The separation between them is  d . A third wire C carrying a current I is to be kept parallel to them at a distance x from A such that the net force acting on it is zero. The possible values of x are:
question: Two wires A &  B are carrying currents I1 and I2 as shown in the figure. The separation between them is  d . A third wire C carrying a current I is to be kept parallel to them at a distance x from A such that the net force acting on it is zero. The possible values of x are:
solution:
As current in A and B are opposite in direction, net force on C can never be zero if it is kept between
A and B .

∴ μ0I1I2πx=μ0I I22πx±d
I1x±d=I2 x
I2-I1x=± I1 d
x=±I1 dI2-I1

subject: Physics
q: The dipole moment of a circular loop carrying a current I, is m and the magnetic field at the centre of the loop is B1 . When the dipole moment is doubled by keeping the current constant, the magnetic field at the centre of the loop is B2  . The ratio B1B2 is:
question: The dipole moment of a circular loop carrying a current I, is m and the magnetic field at the centre of the loop is B1 . When the dipole moment is doubled by keeping the current constant, the magnetic field at the centre of the loop is B2  . The ratio B1B2 is:
solution:
m=magnetic dipole moment=IA. For Loop1, m=Iπr2, B1=μ0i2r
For Loop2. If current is constant m is doubled.  r 1 = 2 r .  B 2 = μ0 i 2 2 r .B1B2=2

q: A rigid wire consists of a semicircular portion of radius R and two straight sections. The wire is partially immerged in a perpendicular magnetic field B=B0 j^ as shown in figure. The magnetic force on the wire if it has a current i is :
question: A rigid wire consists of a semicircular portion of radius R and two straight sections. The wire is partially immerged in a perpendicular magnetic field B=B0 j^ as shown in figure. The magnetic force on the wire if it has a current i is :
solution:
Direction of magnetic field is in +k^

Force acting on wire is given by, F→=il→×B→

As, l=2R, we get

⇒F→=-2iRB j^

subject: Physics
q: A particle having charge $10^{-9}$ C moving in $x$-$y$ plane in fields of $0.4\hat{j}$ N/C and $4 \times 10^{-3} \hat{k}$ T experiences a force of $(4\hat{i} + 2\hat{j}) \times 10^{-10}$ N. The velocity of the particle at that instant is _______ m/s.
question: A particle having charge $10^{-9}$ C moving in $x$-$y$ plane in fields of $0.4\hat{j}$ N/C and $4 \times 10^{-3} \hat{k}$ T experiences a force of $(4\hat{i} + 2\hat{j}) \times 10^{-10}$ N. The velocity of the particle at that instant is _______ m/s.
solution:
The Lorentz force experienced by a charged particle is given by $\vec{F} = q(\vec{E} + \vec{v} \times \vec{B})$.

Given that the particle moves in the $x$-$y$ plane, its velocity can be written as $\vec{v} = v_x \hat{i} + v_y \hat{j}$.

Substituting the given values into the force equation:

$\vec{F} = 10^{-9} [0.4\hat{j} + (v_x \hat{i} + v_y \hat{j}) \times (4 \times 10^{-3} \hat{k})]$

Using the cross product rules $\hat{i} \times \hat{k} = -\hat{j}$ and $\hat{j} \times \hat{k} = \hat{i}$:

$\vec{F} = 10^{-9} [0.4\hat{j} - 4 \times 10^{-3} v_x \hat{j} + 4 \times 10^{-3} v_y \hat{i}]$

$\vec{F} = 10^{-9} [4 \times 10^{-3} v_y \hat{i} + (0.4 - 4 \times 10^{-3} v_x) \hat{j}]$

The given force is $\vec{F} = (4\hat{i} + 2\hat{j}) \times 10^{-10}$ N, which can be rewritten as:

$\vec{F} = 10^{-9} (0.4\hat{i} + 0.2\hat{j})$ N

Comparing the coefficients of $\hat{i}$ and $\hat{j}$ from both expressions:

For the $\hat{i}$ component:
$4 \times 10^{-3} v_y = 0.4$
$v_y = \dfrac{0.4}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 100$ m/s

For the $\hat{j}$ component:
$0.4 - 4 \times 10^{-3} v_x = 0.2$
$4 \times 10^{-3} v_x = 0.2$
$v_x = \dfrac{0.2}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 50$ m/s

Therefore, the velocity of the particle is $\vec{v} = 50\hat{i} + 100\hat{j}$ m/s.

Answer: $50\hat{i} + 100\hat{j}$

subject: Physics
q: Two long parallel wires carrying currents <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> in opposite directions are placed at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from each other. A point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is at equidistant from both the wires such that the lines joining the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> to the wires are perpendicular to each other. The magnitude of magnetic field at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. (Given: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn></math>)
question: Two long parallel wires carrying currents <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> in opposite directions are placed at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from each other. A point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is at equidistant from both the wires such that the lines joining the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> to the wires are perpendicular to each other. The magnitude of magnetic field at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. (Given: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn></math>)
solution:
Here, the magnetic fields produced by the two wires will be perpendicular to one another.

Magnetic field due to wire carrying current 8 A is B1=μ0i12πd

and magnetic field due to wire carrying current 15 A is B2=μ0i22πd

The net magnetic field is 

Bnet=B12+B22=μ02πdi12+i22

=4π×10-72π×72×10-2×82+152 d=72 cm

=68×10-6  T.

subject: Physics
q: A loop ABCDA , carrying current $\mathrm{I}=12 \mathrm{~A}$, is placed in a plane, consists of two semi-circular segments of radius $R_1=6 \pi \mathrm{~m}$ and $\mathrm{R}_2=4 \pi \mathrm{~m}$. The magnitude of the resultant magnetic field at center O is $\mathrm{k} \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~T}$. The value of k is ______ (Given $\mu_0=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{Tm} \mathrm{A}^{-1}$)
question: A loop ABCDA , carrying current $\mathrm{I}=12 \mathrm{~A}$, is placed in a plane, consists of two semi-circular segments of radius $R_1=6 \pi \mathrm{~m}$ and $\mathrm{R}_2=4 \pi \mathrm{~m}$. The magnitude of the resultant magnetic field at center O is $\mathrm{k} \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~T}$. The value of k is ______ (Given $\mu_0=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{Tm} \mathrm{A}^{-1}$)
solution:
Magnetic field due to $\mathrm{AB} \& \mathrm{CD}=0$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{B}_0=\left|\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{R}_1}-\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{R}_2}\right| \\ & =\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{4 \mathrm{R}_2}-\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{4 \mathrm{R}_1} \\ & =\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 12}{4}\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi}-\frac{1}{6 \pi}\right) \\ & =12 \pi \times 10^{-7}\left(\frac{1}{12 \pi}\right) \\ & =1 \times 10^{-7}
\end{aligned}$
$K=1$

subject: Physics
q: A moving coil galvanometer has a coil with <math><mn>175</mn></math> turns and area <math><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> It uses a torsion band of torsion constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></math> The coil is placed in a magnetic field B parallel to its plane. The coil deflects by <math><msup><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup></math> for a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> The value of B (in tesla) is approximately:
question: A moving coil galvanometer has a coil with <math><mn>175</mn></math> turns and area <math><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> It uses a torsion band of torsion constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></math> The coil is placed in a magnetic field B parallel to its plane. The coil deflects by <math><msup><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup></math> for a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> The value of B (in tesla) is approximately:
solution:
For a moving coil galvanometer.
τ=iANB=cθ
B=cθiAN=10-610-4×175×10-3×π180
B≈10-3 T

subject: Physics
q: Consider two thin identical conducting wires covered with very thin insulating material. One of the wires is bent into a loop and produces magnetic field $\mathrm{B}_1$, at its centre when a current $\mathrm{I}$ passes through it. The ratio $\mathrm{B}_1: \mathrm{B}_2$ is:
question: Consider two thin identical conducting wires covered with very thin insulating material. One of the wires is bent into a loop and produces magnetic field $\mathrm{B}_1$, at its centre when a current $\mathrm{I}$ passes through it. The ratio $\mathrm{B}_1: \mathrm{B}_2$ is:
solution:
For loop $B=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{nI}}{2 \mathrm{a}}$ where, $a$ is the radius of loop.
Then, $B_1=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 a}$
Now, for coil $\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \mathrm{nA}}{\mathrm{x}^3}$ at the centre $x=$ radius of loop
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{B}_2 &=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \times 3 \times(\mathrm{I} / 3) \times \pi(\mathrm{a} / 3)^2}{(\mathrm{a} / 3)^3} \\
&=\frac{\mu_0 \cdot 3 \mathrm{I}}{2 \mathrm{a}} \\
\therefore & \frac{\mathrm{B}_1}{\mathrm{~B}_2}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I} / 2 \mathrm{a}}{\mu_0 \cdot 3 \mathrm{I} / 2 \mathrm{a}} \\
\mathrm{B}_1: \mathrm{B}_2=1: 3
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: Two <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> long, straight wires, each carrying a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> are kept parallel to each other. If each wire experienced a force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>, then separation between the wires is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: Two <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> long, straight wires, each carrying a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> are kept parallel to each other. If each wire experienced a force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>, then separation between the wires is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Given: F=10-5 N

Force between wires Fl=μ0i1i22πd, where, d is separation between them.

 ⇒10-50.1=2×10-7×25d

⇒d=50×10-3 m=5 cm

subject: Physics
q: Two coaxial solenoids of different radii carry current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> in the same direction. Let <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>F</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mi> </mi></math> be the magnetic force on the inner solenoid due to the outer one and <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>F</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> be the magnetic force on the outer solenoid due to the inner one. Then:
question: Two coaxial solenoids of different radii carry current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> in the same direction. Let <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>F</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mi> </mi></math> be the magnetic force on the inner solenoid due to the outer one and <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>F</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> be the magnetic force on the outer solenoid due to the inner one. Then:
options: <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>F</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mi> </mi></math>  is radially inwards and  <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>F</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mi> </mi></math>  is radially outwards
solution:
S2 is a solenoid with more radius than S1, the magnetic field of the solenoid S2 will be along the axis and constant, so the magnetic force on an element of solenoid S1 will be,

dF→1=il→×B→

dF→1=ilBj^×-i^=iBlk^

Similarly, for just next coil the magnetic field is in same direction, but the current element is in opposite direction, so,

dF→1=ilB-j^×-i^=-iBlk^.

From Symmetry, we can say that the overall force will be cancelled to the whole coil, hence, F→1=0

Similarly, from coil S1 to S2, The magnetic field of the inner solenoid is symmetric about its axis, but not exactly along the axis of the outer solenoid. As the outer solenoid is placed symmetrically the push of any coil will be equal and opposite to the pull of coil placed exactly the opposite distance from the centre. Hence, the net force on the outer coil will be zero, i.e., F→2=0.

subject: Physics
q: A uniform electric field $\vec{E}$ exists between the plates of a charged condenser. A charged particle enters the space between the plates and perpendicular to $\vec{E}$. The path of the particle between the plates is a:
question: A uniform electric field $\vec{E}$ exists between the plates of a charged condenser. A charged particle enters the space between the plates and perpendicular to $\vec{E}$. The path of the particle between the plates is a:
solution: When charged particle enters perpendicularly in an electric field, it describes a parabolic path $$ \mathrm{y}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\mathrm{QE}}{\mathrm{m}}\right)\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}}{4}\right)^2 $$ This is the equation of parabola.

subject: Physics
q: A $5$ mg particle carrying a charge of $5\pi\times 10^{-6}$ C is moving with velocity of $(3\hat{i}+2\hat{k})\times 10^{-2}$ m/s in a region having magnetic field $\vec{B} = 0.1\hat{k}$ Wb/m$^2$. It moves a distance of $\alpha$ meter along $\hat{k}$ when it completes $5$ revolutions. The value of $\alpha$ is ________.
question: A $5$ mg particle carrying a charge of $5\pi\times 10^{-6}$ C is moving with velocity of $(3\hat{i}+2\hat{k})\times 10^{-2}$ m/s in a region having magnetic field $\vec{B} = 0.1\hat{k}$ Wb/m$^2$. It moves a distance of $\alpha$ meter along $\hat{k}$ when it completes $5$ revolutions. The value of $\alpha$ is ________.
solution:
Given:
Mass of the particle, $m = 5 \text{ mg} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg}$
Charge, $q = 5\pi \times 10^{-6} \text{ C}$
Velocity, $\vec{v} = (3\hat{i} + 2\hat{k}) \times 10^{-2} \text{ m/s}$
Magnetic field, $\vec{B} = 0.1\hat{k} \text{ Wb/m}^2$

The velocity component parallel to the magnetic field is $v_{\parallel} = 2 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m/s}$.

The time period of one revolution is given by:
$T = \dfrac{2\pi m}{qB}$

Substituting the given values:
$T = \dfrac{2\pi \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{5\pi \times 10^{-6} \times 0.1} = \dfrac{2}{0.1} = 20 \text{ s}$

The time taken to complete $5$ revolutions is:
$t = 5T = 5 \times 20 = 100 \text{ s}$

The distance moved along the $\hat{k}$ direction (pitch for $5$ revolutions) is:
$\alpha = v_{\parallel} \times t$
$\alpha = 2 \times 10^{-2} \times 100 = 2 \text{ m}$

Answer: $2$

subject: Physics
q: A square loop of edge length $2 \mathrm{~m}$ carrying current of $2 \mathrm{~A}$ is placed with its edges parallel to the $x$ $y$ axis. A magnetic field is passing through the $x-y$ plane and expressed as $\vec{B}=B_0(1+4 x) \hat{k}$, where $B_0=5 \mathrm{~T}$. The net magnetic force experienced by the loop is _______ $\mathrm{N}$.
question: A square loop of edge length $2 \mathrm{~m}$ carrying current of $2 \mathrm{~A}$ is placed with its edges parallel to the $x$ $y$ axis. A magnetic field is passing through the $x-y$ plane and expressed as $\vec{B}=B_0(1+4 x) \hat{k}$, where $B_0=5 \mathrm{~T}$. The net magnetic force experienced by the loop is _______ $\mathrm{N}$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{B}(\mathrm{x}=0)=\mathrm{B}_0, \quad \mathrm{~B}(\mathrm{x}=2)=9 \mathrm{~B}_0 \\ & \text { Also, } \mathrm{F}=\mathrm{i} \ell \mathrm{B} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{F}_1=\mathrm{i} \ell \mathrm{B}_0 \quad \& \quad \mathrm{~F}_2=9 \mathrm{i} \ell \mathrm{B}_0 \\ & \mathrm{~F}=\mathrm{F}_2-\mathrm{F}_1=8 \mathrm{i} \ell \mathrm{B}_0=8 \times 2 \times 2 \times 5 \\ & \mathrm{~F}=160 \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A magnetic dipole experiences a torque of $80 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}$ when placed in uniform magnetic field in such a way that dipole moment makes angle of $60^{\circ}$ with magnetic field. The potential energy of the dipole is :
question: A magnetic dipole experiences a torque of $80 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{~m}$ when placed in uniform magnetic field in such a way that dipole moment makes angle of $60^{\circ}$ with magnetic field. The potential energy of the dipole is :
solution:
$\tau=\mathrm{M} \times \mathrm{B}=\mathrm{MB} \sin 60=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \mathrm{MB}=80 \sqrt{3}$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{MB}=160 \\ & \mathrm{U}=-\mathrm{M} \cdot \mathrm{B}=-\mathrm{MB} \cos 60 \\ & \mathrm{U}=-160 \times 1 / 2=-80 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Two long current carrying conductors are placed parallel to each other at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> between them. The magnitude of magnetic field produced at mid-point between the two conductors due to current flowing in them is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μT</mi></math>. The equal current flowing in the two conductors is :
question: Two long current carrying conductors are placed parallel to each other at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> between them. The magnitude of magnetic field produced at mid-point between the two conductors due to current flowing in them is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μT</mi></math>. The equal current flowing in the two conductors is :
solution:
Magnetic field in parallel wires is given by B=μ0I2πd

At midway, distance=d2

∵ μ0I2πd2×2=B

⇒μ0π×2Id=B

⇒4×10-7×2I0.08=300×10-6

⇒I=3×10-4×0.082×4×10-7=30 A

As Bnet≠0, so the wires are carrying current in opposite direction.

subject: Physics
q: A current I flows in an infinitely long wire with cross section in the form of a semicircular ring of radius $\mathrm{R}$. The magnitude of the magnetic induction along its axis is
question: A current I flows in an infinitely long wire with cross section in the form of a semicircular ring of radius $\mathrm{R}$. The magnitude of the magnetic induction along its axis is
solution:
$\mathrm{d} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{di}}{2 \pi \mathrm{R}}[-\cos \theta \hat{\mathbf{i}}-\sin \theta \hat{\mathbf{j}}]$ $\mathrm{di}=\frac{\mathrm{T}}{\pi \mathrm{R}} \mathrm{Rd} \theta$ $=\frac{\mathrm{I}}{\pi} \mathrm{d} \theta$ $\mathrm{d} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{2 \pi^2 \mathrm{R}}(-\cos \theta \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\sin \theta \hat{\mathrm{j}})$
$\vec{B}=-\frac{\mu_0 I}{\pi^2 R} \hat{j}$

subject: Physics
q: A coil in the shape of an equilateral triangle of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> lies in a vertical plane between the pole pieces of permanent magnet producing a horizontal magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mi>mT</mi></math>. The torque acting on the coil when a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is passed through it and its plane becomes parallel to the magnetic field will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mi>Nm</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
question: A coil in the shape of an equilateral triangle of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> lies in a vertical plane between the pole pieces of permanent magnet producing a horizontal magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mi>mT</mi></math>. The torque acting on the coil when a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is passed through it and its plane becomes parallel to the magnetic field will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mi>Nm</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
solution:
τ=M→×B→

=IAB

=0.2×3a24×2×10-2=0.2×3×10-2×2×10-24

=3×10-5 N m

subject: Physics
q: Currents of a 10 ampere and 2 ampere are passed through two parallel thin wires $A$ and $B$ respectively in opposite directions. Wire $A$ is infinitely long and the length of the wire $B$ is $2 \mathrm{~m}$. The force acting on the conductor $B$, which is situated at $10 \mathrm{~cm}$ distance from $A$ will be
question: Currents of a 10 ampere and 2 ampere are passed through two parallel thin wires $A$ and $B$ respectively in opposite directions. Wire $A$ is infinitely long and the length of the wire $B$ is $2 \mathrm{~m}$. The force acting on the conductor $B$, which is situated at $10 \mathrm{~cm}$ distance from $A$ will be
solution:
Force acting on conductor $B$ due to conductor $A$ is given by relation $F=\frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2 l}{2 \pi r}$
$l$-length of conductor $B$
$r$-distance between two conductors
$$
\therefore F=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10 \times 2 \times 2}{2 \times \pi \times 0.1}=8 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~N}
$$

subject: Physics
q: An electron is constrained to move along the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi></math> (c is the speed of light) in the presence of electromagnetic wave, whose electric field is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>30</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> in in seconds and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is im meters.The maximum magnetic force experienced by the electron will be: (given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and electron charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>19</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Coloumbs</mi></math>
question: An electron is constrained to move along the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi></math> (c is the speed of light) in the presence of electromagnetic wave, whose electric field is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>30</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> in in seconds and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is im meters.The maximum magnetic force experienced by the electron will be: (given <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and electron charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>19</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Coloumbs</mi></math>
solution:
In electromagnetic wave is E0B0=C
so maximum value of magnetic field is

B0=E0C

Fmax=qVBmax·sin90°

=qV0E0C N

1.6×10-19×0.1×3×108×303×108=4.8×10-19 N

subject: Physics
q: A long solenoid has 200 turns per $\mathrm{cm}$ and carries a current $\mathrm{i}$. The magnetic field at its centre is $6.28 \times 10^{-2}$ Weber $/ \mathrm{m}^2$. Another long solenoid has 100 turns per $\mathrm{cm}$ and it carries a current $\mathrm{i} / 3$. The value of the magnetic field at its centre is
question: A long solenoid has 200 turns per $\mathrm{cm}$ and carries a current $\mathrm{i}$. The magnetic field at its centre is $6.28 \times 10^{-2}$ Weber $/ \mathrm{m}^2$. Another long solenoid has 100 turns per $\mathrm{cm}$ and it carries a current $\mathrm{i} / 3$. The value of the magnetic field at its centre is
solution: $B_2=\frac{B_1 n_2 i_2}{n_1 i_1}=\frac{\left(6.28 \times 10^{-2}\right)(100 \times i / 3)}{200(i)}=1.05 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}^2$

subject: Physics
q: A wire carrying current I is bent in the shapeABCDEFA as shown, where rectangle ABCDA and ADEFA are perpendicular to each other. If the sides of the rectangles are of lengths a and b, then the magnitude and direction of magnetic moment of the loop ABCDEFA is :
question: A wire carrying current I is bent in the shapeABCDEFA as shown, where rectangle ABCDA and ADEFA are perpendicular to each other. If the sides of the rectangles are of lengths a and b, then the magnitude and direction of magnetic moment of the loop ABCDEFA is :
solution:
Loop ABCD

M→1=(abI)(k^)
For Loop DEFA M→2=(abI)(j^)
M→=M→1+M→2;M→=(abI)(j^+k^)
2abI along j^2+k^2.

subject: Physics
q: The magnetic field at the centre of a circular coil of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>, due to current I flowing through it, is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>. The magnetic field at a point along the axis at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> from the centre is :
question: The magnetic field at the centre of a circular coil of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math>, due to current I flowing through it, is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>. The magnetic field at a point along the axis at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> from the centre is :
solution:
Magnetic field at the centre of the circle is, B=μ0i2R

Magnetic field at the point on the axis is, 

B2=μ0iR22R2+x232⇒B2=μ0iR22R2+R2232⇒B2=μ0i25432R=253B

subject: Physics
q: Two identical long current carrying wires are bent into the shapes shown in the following figures. If the magnitude of magnetic fields at the centres P and Q of a semicircular arc are $B_1$ and $B_2$ respectively, then the ratio $\dfrac{B_1}{B_2}$ is _______.
question: Two identical long current carrying wires are bent into the shapes shown in the following figures. If the magnitude of magnetic fields at the centres P and Q of a semicircular arc are $B_1$ and $B_2$ respectively, then the ratio $\dfrac{B_1}{B_2}$ is _______.
solution:
For the first figure, the magnetic field at the centre P is the vector sum of the magnetic fields due to two semi-infinite straight wires and one semicircular arc.

The magnetic field due to the top semi-infinite wire at P is $B_{1a} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi r}$ directed into the plane.

The magnetic field due to the bottom semi-infinite wire at P is $B_{1b} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi r}$ directed into the plane.

The magnetic field due to the semicircular arc at P is $B_{1c} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4r} = \dfrac{\pi \mu_0 I}{4\pi r}$ directed into the plane.

The total magnetic field at P is $B_1 = B_{1a} + B_{1b} + B_{1c} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi r} (1 + 1 + \pi) = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi r} (2 + \pi)$.

For the second figure, the magnetic field at the centre Q is the vector sum of the magnetic fields due to one horizontal semi-infinite wire, one semicircular arc, and one vertical semi-infinite wire.

The magnetic field due to the horizontal semi-infinite wire at Q is $B_{2a} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi r}$ directed into the plane.

The magnetic field due to the semicircular arc at Q is $B_{2c} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4r} = \dfrac{\pi \mu_0 I}{4\pi r}$ directed into the plane.

Since the arc is semicircular, it ends at a point directly below Q. The vertical semi-infinite wire starts from this point and extends downwards, meaning its line of action passes through Q. Therefore, the magnetic field due to this vertical wire at Q is $B_{2b} = 0$.

The total magnetic field at Q is $B_2 = B_{2a} + B_{2b} + B_{2c} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi r} (1 + 0 + \pi) = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi r} (1 + \pi)$.

The ratio of the magnetic fields is $\dfrac{B_1}{B_2} = \dfrac{\dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi r} (2 + \pi)}{\dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi r} (1 + \pi)} = \dfrac{2 + \pi}{1 + \pi}$.

Answer: $\dfrac{2 + \pi}{1 + \pi}$

subject: Physics
q: A circular coil having <math><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> turns and radius <math><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></math> carries a current <math><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mo>.</mo></math> It is held in the <math><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></math> plane in a magnetic field <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> . The torque on the coil due to the magnetic field is:
question: A circular coil having <math><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> turns and radius <math><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></math> carries a current <math><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi><mo>.</mo></math> It is held in the <math><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></math> plane in a magnetic field <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> . The torque on the coil due to the magnetic field is:
solution:
Magnetic moment M→=NIπr2j^
Torque τ→=M→×B→
=NIπr2j^×(Bi^)
=BNI πr2 (-k^) N m
τ→=BNI πr2 N m
